book_claims.claim: Exceptionally rich source of vitamin B12 (100g clams = up to 49 mcg B12)
book_claims.mechanism: Marine organisms accumulate B12 from bacteria in aquatic environments

supplementary_research.finding: Shellfish consumption is inversely associated with cardiovascular disease risk, partly through provision of omega-3 fatty acids, B12, selenium, and zinc (Rimm et al., 2018).
Cooked clams provide 98.9 mcg B12 per 100g (4,120% DV), confirming their status as the single richest natural food source of vitamin B12.

Systematic review (Watanabe et al., 2014) confirms bivalve mollusks (clams, mussels, oysters) as the most bioavailable dietary source of vitamin B12, with absorption rates superior to supplements in food-bound form.

culinary_pairings.source: Acid brightens shellfish flavor and aids iron absorption

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highly bioavailable in food-bound form; superior to synthetic supplements for absorption.
Heme iron from shellfish is 2-3x more bioavailable than plant-based non-heme iron.
Selenomethionine form in shellfish is well absorbed; cofactor for glutathione peroxidase antioxidant system.

synergies.description: One of the richest natural food sources of B12
